summary:
The correct answer is D—tropicamide because it antagonizes muscarinic receptors on the sphincter pupillae, thereby removing tonic inhibition normally exerted by cholinergic pathways; thus mimicking the functional outcome seen when pilocarpie stimulates these muscles through activation instead of blockage—analogous mechanism despite opposite directions due to common site-of-action and pharmacological class within the autonomic nervous system.

Final Answer: D

The query seeks a drug whose effect parallels pilocarpine's action on sphincter pupillae regarding mechanistic similarity—not just similar outcomes like miosis or mydriasis—but specifically involving identical physiological targets and receptor systems. Pilocarpine directly stimulates muscarinic receptors in the sphincter pupillae causing contraction/miotic response. In contrast, tropicamide functions as a competitive antagonist at those very same muscarinic sites, reducing stimulatory influence which results in reduced constriction allowing expansion from other factors such as dilator pupillae muscle activity—a reciprocal yet structurally equivalent intervention based upon shared molecular target engagement across different modes of modulation (agonism vs antagomism). This makes its mode of operation most closely aligned clinically and physiologically with pilocarpine even though they produce divergent effects under normal conditions since each represents distinct
